codeSnapshot.label: cross_entropy_loss.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# Multi-class cross-entropy (PyTorch)
# Input: raw logits (no softmax applied)
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
logits = torch.tensor([[2.0, 1.0, 0.1],   # sample 1
                       [0.5, 2.5, 0.3]])   # sample 2
labels = torch.tensor([0, 1])              # true classes
loss = criterion(logits, labels)
print(f"CE Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")  # ~0.4971

# Manual: -log(softmax(logit)[true_class])
probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)
ce_manual = -torch.log(probs[torch.arange(2), labels]).mean()
print(f"Manual CE: {ce_manual.item():.4f}")  # same

# Binary cross-entropy
bce = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()
y_logit = torch.tensor([1.5, -0.5, 2.0])
y_true = torch.tensor([1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
print(f"BCE Loss: {bce(y_logit, y_true).item():.4f}")

# Label smoothing built in to CrossEntropyLoss
smoothed = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)
print(f"Smoothed CE: {smoothed(logits, labels).item():.4f}")

eli3: Cross-entropy loss asks: 'How surprised is my model when it sees the true answer?' If the model said class A had 90% probability and class A was indeed correct, it's not surprised — low loss. If it gave class A 1% probability and class A was correct, it's very surprised — high loss.
Mathematically it's −log(predicted probability of the true class). Log makes surprises compound: being 10× less confident costs you not twice but log(10) ≈ 2.3 times more loss.

It's the standard loss for classification because it perfectly aligns with maximum likelihood estimation and produces strong, non-vanishing gradients — unlike MSE on probabilities, which causes the model to learn slowly when predictions are very wrong.

whenWhy.title: Multi-class classification (softmax output)
whenWhy.body: For K classes, apply softmax to logits to get probabilities p̂, then L = −log p̂_{true_class}. Cross-entropy with softmax is numerically stable when computed jointly (log-sum-exp trick). PyTorch's nn.CrossEntropyLoss takes raw logits and handles this internally. Every image classifier (ResNet, ViT, EfficientNet) uses this loss.

whenWhy.title: Binary classification (sigmoid output)
whenWhy.body: Binary CE: L = −[y log(p) + (1−y) log(1−p)]. Use BCEWithLogitsLoss (applies sigmoid + CE in one numerically stable step). Used in multi-label classification where each label is independent — not softmax but independent sigmoid per label.

whenWhy.title: Language model training (next-token prediction)
whenWhy.body: GPT and other causal LMs minimise the average cross-entropy over all token positions: L = −(1/T) Σₜ log p_θ(wₜ|w₁,...,wₜ₋₁). This is the standard pre-training objective for all autoregressive LLMs. Perplexity = exp(CE) is the standard reporting metric.

deepDiveIntro: For a K-class classification problem with one-hot label y ∈ {0,1}^K and predicted probabilities p̂ = softmax(z): CE = −Σₖ yₖ log p̂ₖ = −log p̂_{true}. Since only the true class has yₖ=1, only one term survives. The gradient of CE + softmax with respect to logit zⱼ is (p̂ⱼ − yⱼ) — clean, linear, and non-vanishing even when the model is confident and wrong. [1]

Cross-entropy equals KL divergence plus the entropy of the true distribution: H(y, p̂) = H(y) + D_KL(y||p̂). Since H(y) = 0 for one-hot labels, minimising CE is equivalent to minimising KL(y||p̂). This is also equivalent to maximising the log-likelihood of the data under a categorical model: L_CE = −Σᵢ log p̂(yᵢ|xᵢ). [1][2]

deepDive.heading: Gradient of CE + Softmax
deepDive.body: For logit vector z with softmax p̂ₖ = exp(zₖ)/Σⱼ exp(zⱼ), the gradient of L = −log p̂_c (true class c) with respect to zⱼ is: ∂L/∂zⱼ = p̂ⱼ − 1{j=c}. This is simply (prediction − label). This clean gradient avoids the vanishing gradient problem that plagues MSE + sigmoid. It is the primary reason cross-entropy is preferred over MSE for classification. [1]

deepDive.heading: Numerical Stability: Log-Sum-Exp Trick
deepDive.body: Computing log(softmax(z)) naively: exp(z) can overflow for large logits. The numerically stable version subtracts max(z) first: log p̂ₖ = zₖ − log(Σⱼ exp(zⱼ − max(z))) − max(z). This doesn't change the result but prevents overflow. PyTorch's F.cross_entropy and nn.LogSoftmax implement this. Never apply softmax then log separately — precision loss compounds. [1]

deepDive.heading: Label Smoothing
deepDive.body: Hard one-hot targets push the model to predict extreme probabilities, causing overconfidence. Label smoothing replaces target y_c = 1 with y_c = 1 − ε, distributing ε/(K−1) to other classes. Smoothed CE = −[(1−ε) log p̂_c + (ε/(K−1)) Σ_{k≠c} log p̂_k]. Used in training transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017: ε=0.1) and image classifiers (EfficientNet). Reduces calibration error and improves generalisation. [2]

deepDive.heading: Focal Loss: Addressing Class Imbalance
deepDive.body: RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017) introduced focal loss to handle extreme foreground/background imbalance in object detection: FL = −(1−p̂_c)^γ log p̂_c, γ ≥ 0. The modulating factor (1−p̂_c)^γ down-weights easy examples (high p̂_c → small factor) so the model focuses on hard, misclassified examples. At γ=0 it reduces to CE; typical γ=2 works well. Reduces the effective loss from the 99.9% easy negatives by ~100×. [3]

takeaways: L = −log p̂_{true class} — penalises low probability assigned to the correct class. [1]
CE = KL(y||p̂) for one-hot labels — minimising CE ≡ maximising log-likelihood. [1]
Label smoothing (ε=0.1) reduces overconfidence and improves calibration. [2]
Focal loss = CE weighted by (1−p̂)^γ — targets hard examples in imbalanced datasets. [3]

edgeCases.q: Why does CE produce better gradients than MSE for classification?
edgeCases.a: With MSE and sigmoid: ∂L/∂z = (ŷ−y)·σ'(z) = (ŷ−y)·ŷ(1−ŷ). When a confident wrong prediction saturates the sigmoid (ŷ≈0 or 1), σ'(z)≈0 and the gradient vanishes — learning stalls. With CE and softmax: ∂L/∂z = ŷ−y, no saturation term. Even if the model is confidently wrong (e.g., class 0 gets 99% but true class is 1), the gradient is large, enabling rapid correction.

edgeCases.q: What is temperature scaling in the context of cross-entropy?
edgeCases.a: Temperature T divides logits before softmax: p̂ₖ = exp(zₖ/T)/Σⱼ exp(zⱼ/T). High T (>1) makes the distribution more uniform (lower confidence); low T (<1) makes it more peaked (higher confidence). In knowledge distillation, teacher's soft labels at high T carry more information about similarities between classes than hard one-hot labels. In inference, temperature controls exploration vs exploitation (used in LLM sampling). Temperature does not change the argmax at T=1 but affects cross-entropy loss value.

sources.label: Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville — Deep Learning, Ch. 6
Vaswani et al. (2017) — Attention Is All You Need arXiv:1706.03762
Lin et al. (2017) — Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection arXiv:1708.02002
